questions.q: What is the primary advantage of transfer learning in image classification?
options: It requires more training data than training from scratch
It allows reusing pre-trained features, reducing training time and improving accuracy with limited data
It always produces better results regardless of dataset size
It eliminates the need for labeled data
questions.explanation: Transfer learning leverages features learned from large datasets (like ImageNet) and adapts them to new tasks, saving computation and requiring less domain-specific data. Training from scratch often needs more data and computational resources.

questions.q: What is ImageNet's primary role in deep learning?
options: A cloud service for hosting models
A large-scale labeled dataset used to pre-train CNNs
A type of neural network architecture
A library for image augmentation
questions.explanation: ImageNet contains over 14 million labeled images across thousands of categories, serving as the standard benchmark for training deep CNN models like ResNet and VGG before fine-tuning on specific tasks.

questions.q: In data augmentation, what is the purpose of random rotation during training?
options: To reduce file size of training images
To teach the model to handle objects at different orientations and improve generalization
To speed up training time
To increase the brightness of dark images
questions.explanation: Random rotation creates synthetic training variations, making the model invariant to object orientation. This improves robustness on test data with different viewing angles. Augmentation increases diversity, not efficiency.

questions.q: What does 'overfitting' mean in the context of image classification models?
options: The model fails to learn any useful patterns
The model memorizes training data details instead of learning generalizable features
The model trains too quickly
The model uses too many augmentation techniques
questions.explanation: Overfitting occurs when a model learns training-specific details and noise rather than generalizable patterns, causing poor performance on unseen test data. This happens with insufficient regularization or too-complex models.

questions.q: How does a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) differ from a fully connected network for image classification?
options: CNNs use more layers and are always faster
CNNs use convolutional layers to learn local spatial features efficiently through parameter sharing
Fully connected networks are superior for image data
CNNs cannot process color images
questions.explanation: CNNs exploit image spatial structure using local convolutions with weight sharing, dramatically reducing parameters and learning shift-invariant features. Fully connected networks treat each pixel independently, ignoring spatial relationships.

questions.q: What is the purpose of batch normalization in CNN training?
options: To shuffle image pixels randomly
To normalize neuron activations during training, improving learning stability and speed
To compress the model size
To increase the number of trainable parameters
questions.explanation: Batch normalization standardizes layer inputs by normalizing activations across mini-batches, reducing internal covariate shift. This accelerates training and allows higher learning rates without divergence.

questions.q: In fine-tuning a pre-trained ImageNet model, why might you freeze early layers?
options: To reduce memory usage during inference
Early layers learn generic low-level features (edges, textures) that transfer well; fine-tuning only later task-specific layers conserves training resources
To prevent the model from learning anything
To increase the model's depth
questions.explanation: Early convolutional layers in ImageNet models learn universally useful features. Freezing them and only training later layers for your specific task is efficient and works well with limited domain-specific data.

questions.q: What is 'class imbalance' in image classification and why is it problematic?
options: When the model uses too many classes
When some classes have far fewer training examples than others, causing bias toward majority classes
When training images have different resolutions
When augmentation is not applied to images
questions.explanation: Class imbalance causes models to learn majority class patterns while neglecting minority classes, resulting in poor minority-class performance. This requires techniques like weighted loss functions or oversampling.

questions.q: What does the softmax function do in multi-class image classification?
options: It converts raw model outputs to normalized probability scores across all classes
It selects the class with the largest activations
It measures model accuracy on test data
It applies color filtering to input images
questions.explanation: Softmax normalizes the final layer outputs into a probability distribution where all class probabilities sum to 1, allowing interpretation as class likelihoods. The predicted class is the one with maximum probability.

questions.q: Why is cross-entropy loss commonly used for training image classification models?
options: It's faster to compute than other loss functions
It measures the distance between predicted probability distributions and ground truth, providing meaningful gradients for classification tasks
It only works with binary classification
It automatically handles class imbalance
questions.explanation: Cross-entropy quantifies how well predicted probabilities match true labels and provides strong gradients for backpropagation. It's theoretically justified for probability-based classification and more effective than L2 loss for this task.
